title: Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)
intent_tags: how much is a customer worth
how much does a customer spend over time
when_to_use: determining how much to spend to acquire a customer
comparing customer value across acquisition channels
segmenting customers by long-term revenue contribution
evaluating whether LTV:CAC ratio is healthy
projecting future revenue from existing customer base
prioritizing retention investment by customer segment
business_definition: Total net revenue generated by a customer across all orders from their first purchase to the present (historical CLV), or projected over a defined time horizon using average order frequency and retention rate (predictive CLV). Excludes cancelled, test, and fraud-flagged orders. Calculated per customer, then averaged or segmented.

metric_nature.what_it_measures: How much total revenue a customer generates over their relationship with the business. Used to answer: can we afford this acquisition cost, and which customers are most valuable?
metric_nature.what_it_does_not_measure: Customer profitability. CLV is revenue-based and does not subtract COGS, fulfillment costs, or support costs. Use contribution-margin-adjusted CLV for profitability analysis.
metric_nature.common_misconception: CLV is often confused with average revenue per customer in a single period. A customer who spent $200 once is not the same as one who spent $50 four times — the latter has higher predicted future CLV because they demonstrated repeat behavior.

template:
WITH customer_orders AS (
  SELECT
    o.customer_id,
    COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS order_count,
    SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS total_revenue,
    MIN(o.order_date) AS first_order_date,
    MAX(o.order_date) AS last_order_date
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
    AND o.order_date BETWEEN {start_date} AND {end_date}
  GROUP BY o.customer_id
)
SELECT
  AVG(total_revenue) AS avg_clv,
  PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY total_revenue) AS median_clv,
  AVG(order_count) AS avg_orders_per_customer
FROM customer_orders

inflation_causes.cause: Including cancelled or refunded orders in lifetime total
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If cancelled orders are not filtered, the SUM includes revenue that was never fulfilled. A customer who placed 5 orders but cancelled 3 shows CLV of 5 orders instead of 2, overstating their actual value by up to 2.5x.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT o.customer_id, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS total_orders, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.status = 'cancelled' THEN o.order_id END) AS cancelled_orders, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS raw_total, SUM(CASE WHEN o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') THEN oi.quantity * oi.unit_price ELSE 0 END) AS filtered_total FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id GROUP BY o.customer_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.status = 'cancelled' THEN o.order_id END) > 0 ORDER BY raw_total - filtered_total DESC LIMIT 20
inflation_causes.fix: Add WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') to the customer revenue aggregation. Apply this filter in the base dbt model so all downstream CLV calculations inherit it.

inflation_causes.cause: Counting gross revenue instead of net revenue
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If CLV sums order_items.quantity * unit_price without subtracting refunds and discounts, the lifetime value reflects what was charged, not what was retained. For businesses with 15-20% return rates, this inflates CLV by the same percentage.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT c.customer_id, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS gross_clv, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) - COALESCE(SUM(r.refund_amount), 0) AS net_clv, COALESCE(SUM(r.refund_amount), 0) AS total_refunds FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id LEFT JOIN refunds r ON o.order_id = r.order_id JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY c.customer_id HAVING COALESCE(SUM(r.refund_amount), 0) > 0 ORDER BY total_refunds DESC LIMIT 20
inflation_causes.fix: Subtract COALESCE(SUM(r.refund_amount), 0) from the revenue sum, or use a net_revenue column if your data model already computes it at the order level.

inflation_causes.cause: Duplicate customer records inflating per-customer average
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If the customers table has duplicate customer_ids due to email variations (john@gmail.com vs John@Gmail.com) or guest checkout creating new IDs, orders are split across multiple IDs. When averaged, these fragmented customers pull the average DOWN — but the COUNT of customers is inflated, making total CLV * customer_count appear larger than reality.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT LOWER(c.email) AS normalized_email, COUNT(DISTINCT c.customer_id) AS customer_ids, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS total_orders, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS combined_revenue FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY LOWER(c.email) HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT c.customer_id) > 1 ORDER BY combined_revenue DESC LIMIT 20
inflation_causes.fix: Create a canonical customer_id mapping table that merges duplicates by normalized email or phone. Use this resolved ID in all CLV calculations.

inflation_causes.cause: B2B bulk orders mixed with B2C individual orders
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If wholesale or bulk orders (single customer placing $50K+ orders) are averaged with individual consumer orders, a handful of B2B accounts can skew the average CLV dramatically upward, misrepresenting the value of the typical customer.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT customer_id, COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS orders, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS total_revenue, MAX(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS max_single_item_value FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY customer_id ORDER BY total_revenue DESC LIMIT 50
inflation_causes.fix: Segment B2B and B2C customers using an account_type flag or a revenue threshold. Report CLV separately for each segment, or exclude B2B accounts from consumer CLV reporting.

deflation_causes.cause: Guest checkout creating fragmented customer identities
deflation_causes.how_it_deflates: Each guest checkout creates a new customer_id even when the same person orders again. A customer who ordered 5 times as a guest appears as 5 separate single-order customers with CLV equal to one order each, dramatically understating their true lifetime value.
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT CASE WHEN c.is_guest = true THEN 'guest' ELSE 'registered' END AS customer_type, COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id) AS customers, AVG(customer_revenue) AS avg_clv FROM (SELECT o.customer_id, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS customer_revenue FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY o.customer_id) sub JOIN customers c ON sub.customer_id = c.customer_id GROUP BY 1
deflation_causes.fix: Implement identity resolution that maps guest checkouts to registered accounts using email, shipping address, or device fingerprint. Recalculate CLV on the resolved identity.

deflation_causes.cause: Time window too narrow to capture full lifetime
deflation_causes.how_it_deflates: If CLV is calculated using only the last 90 days of data, customers who have been purchasing for years show only their recent orders. This truncates the lifetime and severely underestimates the value of loyal, long-tenured customers.
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('year', MIN(o.order_date)) AS first_order_year, COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id) AS customers, AVG(total_rev) AS avg_clv FROM (SELECT o.customer_id, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS total_rev, MIN(o.order_date) AS first_date FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY o.customer_id) sub JOIN orders o ON sub.customer_id = o.customer_id GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1
deflation_causes.fix: Use the full order history from the customer's first order to the present. If a time window is needed, filter on first_order_date (cohort entry) rather than order_date so each customer's complete history is included.

diagnostic_questions: Is the CLV anomaly in a specific acquisition channel or cohort, or across all customers?
Has the average order frequency changed, or is it the average order value that shifted?
Are guest checkout customers being merged with registered accounts, or counted separately?
Did refund or return rates change in the same period, affecting net revenue per customer?
Is the CLV calculation using gross or net revenue — and which does the business intend?
Has a large B2B account or outlier customer been added or removed from the dataset?

causal_relationships.if: CLV up, ec_repeat_rate flat
causal_relationships.then: average order value per transaction increased rather than purchase frequency — check for price increases or upsell effectiveness

causal_relationships.if: CLV up, ec_aov flat
causal_relationships.then: purchase frequency increased — customers are buying more often at the same basket size, which indicates improved retention or engagement

causal_relationships.if: CLV down, ec_cohort_retention down
causal_relationships.then: customers are churning faster, shortening the active lifetime window and reducing total revenue per customer

causal_relationships.if: CLV up in one channel only
causal_relationships.then: that acquisition channel is attracting higher-quality customers — verify with ec_new_vs_returning segmented by utm_source or channel

cross_metric_signals.and_also: ec_cohort_retention shows D30 retention declining in recent cohorts
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: Recent acquisition cohorts are lower quality — they churn faster and generate fewer repeat orders. The root cause is upstream in the acquisition funnel: a channel mix shift, a promotion that attracted deal-seekers, or a product-market fit regression for new customer segments.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', first_order_date) AS cohort_month, COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS cohort_size, AVG(total_revenue) AS avg_clv, AVG(order_count) AS avg_orders FROM (SELECT o.customer_id, MIN(o.order_date) AS first_order_date, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS total_revenue, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS order_count FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY o.customer_id) cohort_stats GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1 DESC LIMIT 12

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: CLV increases but ec_gmv is flat or declining
cross_metric_signals.and_also: ec_new_vs_returning shows new customer count declining
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: The business is losing new customer acquisition volume. CLV rises because the remaining customer base is increasingly composed of loyal repeat buyers (survivorship bias). Total GMV stalls because new customer inflow has dried up. Check acquisition spend and funnel conversion rates.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS month, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_date = c.first_order_date THEN o.customer_id END) AS new_customers, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_date > c.first_order_date THEN o.customer_id END) AS returning_customers, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS gmv FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id JOIN (SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY customer_id) c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1 DESC LIMIT 12

anti_patterns.why_wrong: AVG(order_total) gives average order value per transaction, not lifetime value per customer. A customer who placed 10 orders of $50 each has CLV of $500 but AVG(order_total) of $50. This confuses AOV with CLV and understates customer value by the average order frequency.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT AVG(o.order_total) AS clv FROM orders o WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
anti_patterns.good_sql: SELECT AVG(customer_total) AS avg_clv FROM (SELECT o.customer_id, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS customer_total FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY o.customer_id) customer_rev

anti_patterns.pattern: Computing CLV without grouping by customer first
anti_patterns.why_wrong: If you SUM revenue across all orders without a subquery that groups by customer_id first, you get total revenue — not per-customer lifetime value. The AVG of this is meaningless because there is only one row.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id), 0) AS clv FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
anti_patterns.good_sql:
WITH customer_revenue AS (
  SELECT o.customer_id, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS lifetime_revenue
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  GROUP BY o.customer_id
)
SELECT AVG(lifetime_revenue) AS avg_clv, PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY lifetime_revenue) AS median_clv FROM customer_revenue

anti_patterns.pattern: Ignoring CLV distribution skew by reporting only the mean
anti_patterns.why_wrong: CLV distributions are heavily right-skewed — a small number of whale customers pull the mean far above the median. Reporting only AVG(clv) gives a number that is not representative of the typical customer and leads to over-investment in acquisition based on inflated unit economics.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT AVG(lifetime_revenue) AS clv FROM customer_revenue_summary
anti_patterns.good_sql: SELECT AVG(lifetime_revenue) AS mean_clv, PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY lifetime_revenue) AS median_clv, PERCENTILE_CONT(0.9) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY lifetime_revenue) AS p90_clv, STDDEV(lifetime_revenue) AS clv_stddev FROM customer_revenue_summary

sql_assets.basic_sql: SELECT AVG(customer_total) AS avg_clv FROM (SELECT o.customer_id, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS customer_total FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') AND o.order_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-12-31' GROUP BY o.customer_id) customer_rev
sql_assets.intermediate_sql: SELECT c.acquisition_channel, COUNT(DISTINCT sub.customer_id) AS customers, AVG(sub.lifetime_revenue) AS avg_clv, PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY sub.lifetime_revenue) AS median_clv, AVG(sub.order_count) AS avg_orders FROM (SELECT o.customer_id, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS lifetime_revenue, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS order_count FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY o.customer_id) sub JOIN customers c ON sub.customer_id = c.customer_id GROUP BY c.acquisition_channel ORDER BY avg_clv DESC
sql_assets.advanced_sql:
WITH customer_orders AS (
  -- Grain: one row per customer with lifetime aggregates
  SELECT
    o.customer_id,
    MIN(o.order_date) AS first_order_date,
    MAX(o.order_date) AS last_order_date,
    COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS order_count,
    SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS gross_revenue,
    SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) - COALESCE(SUM(r.refund_amount), 0) AS net_revenue
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  LEFT JOIN refunds r ON o.order_id = r.order_id
  WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  GROUP BY o.customer_id
),
cohort_clv AS (
  SELECT
    DATE_TRUNC('quarter', first_order_date) AS cohort_quarter,
    COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS cohort_size,
    AVG(net_revenue) AS avg_clv,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY net_revenue) AS median_clv,
    AVG(order_count) AS avg_frequency,
    AVG(net_revenue) / NULLIF(AVG(order_count), 0) AS avg_revenue_per_order
  FROM customer_orders
  GROUP BY 1
)
SELECT
  cohort_quarter,
  cohort_size,
  ROUND(avg_clv, 2) AS avg_clv,
  ROUND(median_clv, 2) AS median_clv,
  ROUND(avg_frequency, 2) AS avg_frequency,
  ROUND(avg_revenue_per_order, 2) AS avg_revenue_per_order,
  ROUND((avg_clv - LAG(avg_clv) OVER (ORDER BY cohort_quarter)) / NULLIF(LAG(avg_clv) OVER (ORDER BY cohort_quarter), 0) * 100, 2) AS clv_qoq_pct
FROM cohort_clv
ORDER BY cohort_quarter

title: Repeat Purchase Rate
intent_tags: what percentage of customers come back
when_to_use: measuring customer loyalty and stickiness
evaluating whether acquisition channels bring repeat buyers
comparing retention performance across product categories
assessing the health of the customer base over time
determining if a promotion drove one-time or repeat behavior
business_definition: Percentage of unique customers who placed more than one order in a defined period. Calculated as COUNT(DISTINCT customers with order_count >= 2) / COUNT(DISTINCT all customers) over the period. Excludes cancelled, test, and fraud-flagged orders.

metric_nature.what_it_measures: The proportion of customers who came back to buy again. Answers: are we building a loyal customer base, or are we a one-time-purchase business?
metric_nature.what_it_does_not_measure: How quickly customers repeat or how much they spend on repeat orders. A 40% repeat rate tells you nothing about whether those repeats happen in 30 days or 300 days. Use ec_cohort_retention for time-bound retention and ec_clv for revenue impact.
metric_nature.common_misconception: Repeat rate is time-window dependent and not comparable across different periods without normalization. A 12-month repeat rate will always be higher than a 3-month repeat rate for the same cohort. Teams often compare these naively and conclude retention is improving.

template:
WITH customer_order_counts AS (
  SELECT
    o.customer_id,
    COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS order_count
  FROM orders o
  WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
    AND o.order_date BETWEEN {start_date} AND {end_date}
  GROUP BY o.customer_id
)
SELECT
  COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS total_customers,
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN order_count >= 2 THEN customer_id END) AS repeat_customers,
  ROUND(COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN order_count >= 2 THEN customer_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id), 0), 2) AS repeat_rate_pct
FROM customer_order_counts

inflation_causes.cause: Counting order modifications or amendments as separate orders
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If a customer modifies an existing order (size change, address update) and the system creates a new order_id for the amendment, that customer appears to have placed 2 orders when they only intended 1. This artificially classifies single-purchase customers as repeat buyers.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT o.customer_id, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS order_count, COUNT(DISTINCT o.original_order_id) AS distinct_originals FROM orders o WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') AND o.original_order_id IS NOT NULL GROUP BY o.customer_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) > COUNT(DISTINCT o.original_order_id) LIMIT 20
inflation_causes.fix: Use the original_order_id (or parent_order_id) to group amendments with their parent order. Count DISTINCT original_order_id instead of order_id when computing purchase frequency.

inflation_causes.cause: Split shipments creating multiple order records
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: When one customer order is fulfilled in multiple shipments and the system creates a separate order_id per shipment, the customer's order count is inflated. A single purchase split into 3 shipments shows as 3 orders, making repeat rate appear higher.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT o.customer_id, o.order_date, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS orders_same_day FROM orders o WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY o.customer_id, o.order_date HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) > 1 ORDER BY orders_same_day DESC LIMIT 20
inflation_causes.fix: Group orders by (customer_id, order_date, shipping_address) or use a cart_id / checkout_session_id to consolidate split shipments into a single logical purchase event.

inflation_causes.cause: Subscription auto-renewals counted as repeat purchases
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If subscription renewal orders (auto-generated monthly) are included in the repeat purchase calculation, every subscriber appears as a repeat customer by definition. This conflates active repurchasing decisions with passive billing events.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT CASE WHEN o.is_subscription = true THEN 'subscription' ELSE 'one_time' END AS order_type, COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id) AS customers, AVG(order_count) AS avg_orders FROM (SELECT customer_id, is_subscription, COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS order_count FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY customer_id, is_subscription) sub GROUP BY 1
inflation_causes.fix: Exclude subscription renewal orders (WHERE is_subscription = false) when calculating organic repeat purchase rate, or report subscription and non-subscription repeat rates separately.

deflation_causes.how_it_deflates: Each guest checkout generates a new customer_id. A customer who bought 3 times as a guest appears as 3 unique single-purchase customers, each with order_count = 1. The repeat rate drops because the denominator inflates with phantom one-time buyers who are actually repeat customers.
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT CASE WHEN c.is_guest = true THEN 'guest' ELSE 'registered' END AS customer_type, COUNT(DISTINCT c.customer_id) AS customers, AVG(sub.order_count) AS avg_orders, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN sub.order_count >= 2 THEN c.customer_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT c.customer_id), 0) AS repeat_rate_pct FROM (SELECT customer_id, COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS order_count FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY customer_id) sub JOIN customers c ON sub.customer_id = c.customer_id GROUP BY 1
deflation_causes.fix: Implement identity resolution using email or shipping address to merge guest and registered profiles before calculating repeat rate.

deflation_causes.how_it_deflates: If repeat rate is calculated over 30 days but the average repurchase cycle is 90 days, most customers who will repeat have not had time to do so yet. The rate appears low not because customers are disloyal, but because the window is shorter than the natural buying cycle.
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT ROUND(AVG(days_to_second_order)) AS avg_days_to_repeat, PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY days_to_second_order) AS median_days_to_repeat FROM (SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_order, MIN(CASE WHEN order_rank = 2 THEN order_date END) AS second_order, MIN(CASE WHEN order_rank = 2 THEN order_date END) - MIN(order_date) AS days_to_second_order FROM (SELECT customer_id, order_date, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date) AS order_rank FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')) ranked GROUP BY customer_id HAVING MIN(CASE WHEN order_rank = 2 THEN order_date END) IS NOT NULL) repeat_buyers
deflation_causes.fix: Set the observation window to at least 2x the median days-to-second-order. Alternatively, use cohort-based repeat rate where you measure repeat behavior for cohorts with enough maturity time.

diagnostic_questions: What time window is this repeat rate calculated over, and is it longer than the typical repurchase cycle?
Are guest checkout customers being resolved to a single identity, or counted as separate customers?
Has the mix of subscription vs one-time orders changed in this period?
Is the repeat rate shift concentrated in a specific acquisition channel or product category?
Did a major promotion or sale event in the prior period create a spike of one-time deal-seekers?

causal_relationships.if: repeat_rate up, ec_clv flat
causal_relationships.then: customers are buying more often but spending less per order — check ec_aov trend to confirm whether basket size is shrinking

causal_relationships.if: repeat_rate down, ec_new_vs_returning shows new customer share increasing
causal_relationships.then: acquisition is outpacing retention — the denominator is growing with new customers who have not yet had time to repeat

causal_relationships.if: repeat_rate up, ec_cohort_retention flat
causal_relationships.then: the improvement may be a time-window artifact — more customers crossed the repeat threshold in this measurement period, but the underlying retention curve has not changed

causal_relationships.if: repeat_rate down across all channels
causal_relationships.then: systemic issue — check for product quality problems, shipping delays (logi_on_time_delivery_rate), or competitive pressure rather than a channel-specific cause

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: Repeat rate drops >5 percentage points QoQ
cross_metric_signals.and_also: ec_clv is also declining in the same cohorts
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: Recent customer cohorts are lower quality — acquired through aggressive discounting or low-intent channels. These customers made a single discounted purchase and never returned. Both metrics decline because the same underlying customer quality problem reduces frequency (repeat rate) and total spend (CLV).
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('quarter', c.first_order_date) AS cohort_quarter, c.acquisition_channel, COUNT(DISTINCT c.customer_id) AS cohort_size, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN sub.order_count >= 2 THEN c.customer_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT c.customer_id), 0) AS repeat_rate_pct, AVG(sub.total_revenue) AS avg_clv FROM (SELECT o.customer_id, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS order_count, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS total_revenue FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY o.customer_id) sub JOIN (SELECT customer_id, acquisition_channel, MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY customer_id, acquisition_channel) c ON sub.customer_id = c.customer_id GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 1 DESC, repeat_rate_pct DESC

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: Repeat rate increases sharply in a single month
cross_metric_signals.and_also: ec_aov drops in the same period
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: A loyalty promotion or repeat-purchase discount (e.g., 20% off your second order) drove customers to place small repeat orders to capture the deal. The repeat count went up, but basket size went down because customers split their purchases to trigger the promotion multiple times.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS month, d.discount_code, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS orders_with_discount, AVG(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS avg_item_value, COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id) AS unique_customers FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id LEFT JOIN discounts d ON o.order_id = d.order_id WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 1 DESC, orders_with_discount DESC LIMIT 20

anti_patterns.pattern: Using COUNT of orders divided by COUNT of customers as repeat rate
anti_patterns.why_wrong: This gives average order frequency, not repeat rate. A business with 1000 customers and 2000 orders has an average frequency of 2.0, but the repeat rate depends on the distribution — it could be 50% at 2 orders each or 10% at 11 orders each. These are very different retention profiles.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) * 1.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id), 0) AS repeat_rate FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
anti_patterns.good_sql:
WITH customer_counts AS (
  SELECT customer_id, COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS order_count
  FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  GROUP BY customer_id
)
SELECT
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN order_count >= 2 THEN customer_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id), 0) AS repeat_rate_pct
FROM customer_counts

anti_patterns.pattern: Comparing repeat rates across different time windows
anti_patterns.why_wrong: A 6-month repeat rate will always be higher than a 3-month repeat rate for the same cohort because customers have more time to make a second purchase. Comparing them without normalizing for window length creates a false impression of improvement or decline.
anti_patterns.bad_sql:
-- Q1 only (3 months)
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN cnt >= 2 THEN customer_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id), 0) FROM (SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM orders WHERE order_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-03-31' AND status NOT IN ('cancelled','test','fraud') GROUP BY customer_id) x
-- vs full year (unfair comparison)
-- SELECT ... WHERE order_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-12-31'
anti_patterns.good_sql:
-- Use cohort-based repeat rate with fixed observation window
WITH cohort AS (
  SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date
  FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  GROUP BY customer_id
),
cohort_repeat AS (
  SELECT c.customer_id, c.first_order_date,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_date BETWEEN c.first_order_date AND c.first_order_date + INTERVAL '90 days' AND o.order_date > c.first_order_date THEN o.order_id END) AS repeat_orders_90d
  FROM cohort c
  LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id AND o.status NOT IN ('cancelled','test','fraud')
  GROUP BY c.customer_id, c.first_order_date
)
SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', first_order_date) AS cohort_month,
  COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS cohort_size,
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN repeat_orders_90d > 0 THEN customer_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id), 0) AS repeat_rate_90d
FROM cohort_repeat
GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1

sql_assets.basic_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN order_count >= 2 THEN customer_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id), 0) AS repeat_rate_pct FROM (SELECT customer_id, COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS order_count FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') AND order_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-12-31' GROUP BY customer_id) customer_counts
sql_assets.intermediate_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('quarter', first_order_date) AS cohort_quarter, COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS cohort_size, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN order_count >= 2 THEN customer_id END) AS repeat_customers, ROUND(COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN order_count >= 2 THEN customer_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id), 0), 2) AS repeat_rate_pct FROM (SELECT o.customer_id, MIN(o.order_date) AS first_order_date, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS order_count FROM orders o WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY o.customer_id) sub GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1
sql_assets.advanced_sql:
WITH customer_orders AS (
  -- Grain: one row per customer with order history
  SELECT
    o.customer_id,
    c.acquisition_channel,
    MIN(o.order_date) AS first_order_date,
    COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS order_count
  FROM orders o
  JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
  WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  GROUP BY o.customer_id, c.acquisition_channel
),
cohort_repeat AS (
  SELECT
    DATE_TRUNC('quarter', first_order_date) AS cohort_quarter,
    acquisition_channel,
    COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS cohort_size,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN order_count >= 2 THEN customer_id END) AS repeat_customers
  FROM customer_orders
  GROUP BY 1, 2
),
with_trend AS (
  SELECT
    cohort_quarter,
    acquisition_channel,
    cohort_size,
    repeat_customers,
    ROUND(repeat_customers * 100.0 / NULLIF(cohort_size, 0), 2) AS repeat_rate_pct,
    LAG(ROUND(repeat_customers * 100.0 / NULLIF(cohort_size, 0), 2)) OVER (PARTITION BY acquisition_channel ORDER BY cohort_quarter) AS prev_quarter_repeat_rate
  FROM cohort_repeat
)
SELECT
  cohort_quarter,
  acquisition_channel,
  cohort_size,
  repeat_customers,
  repeat_rate_pct,
  ROUND(repeat_rate_pct - COALESCE(prev_quarter_repeat_rate, 0), 2) AS repeat_rate_change_pp
FROM with_trend
ORDER BY cohort_quarter DESC, repeat_rate_pct DESC

title: New vs Returning Customer Ratio
when_to_use: understanding the composition of revenue between new and returning customers
evaluating whether growth is driven by acquisition or retention
monitoring the health of the acquisition funnel over time
benchmarking new customer share against industry standards
detecting over-reliance on new customers for revenue
business_definition: Classification of each customer's order as 'new' (their first-ever completed order) or 'returning' (any subsequent completed order), then aggregation of customer counts and revenue by this classification for a period. A customer is 'new' only once — on the date of their first order. Excludes cancelled, test, and fraud-flagged orders.

metric_nature.what_it_measures: The split between first-time buyers and repeat buyers in a given period. Answers: is our revenue growth coming from attracting new people, or from getting existing customers to buy again?
metric_nature.what_it_does_not_measure: Customer quality or retention durability. A high returning share does not mean retention is strong — it could mean acquisition has collapsed. Pair with ec_repeat_rate and ec_cohort_retention for the full picture.
metric_nature.common_misconception: A rising returning-customer share is not always good. It can mask declining new customer acquisition, which eventually causes total revenue to stall as the returning base slowly churns without replacement.

template:
WITH first_orders AS (
  SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date
  FROM orders
  WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  GROUP BY customer_id
)
SELECT
  CASE WHEN o.order_date = f.first_order_date THEN 'new' ELSE 'returning' END AS customer_type,
  COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id) AS customers,
  COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS orders,
  SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue
FROM orders o
JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
JOIN first_orders f ON o.customer_id = f.customer_id
WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  AND o.order_date BETWEEN {start_date} AND {end_date}
GROUP BY 1

inflation_causes.cause: Guest checkout creating false new-customer records
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: Every guest checkout generates a new customer_id. A returning customer who checks out as a guest is classified as 'new' because the system cannot link them to their prior orders. This inflates the new-customer count and understates the returning-customer count.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT CASE WHEN c.is_guest = true THEN 'guest' ELSE 'registered' END AS registration_type, COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id) AS customers, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS orders FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY 1
inflation_causes.fix: Implement identity resolution that maps guest checkouts to existing customer profiles via email, phone, or shipping address before classifying new vs returning.

inflation_causes.cause: Using order_date instead of first_order_date for classification
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If you classify based on whether there are prior orders in the current reporting window rather than across all history, a returning customer whose prior orders fall outside the window is misclassified as new. A customer who first ordered in December appears 'new' in the January report.
inflation_causes.detection_sql:
WITH first_ever AS (
  SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date
  FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  GROUP BY customer_id
),
first_in_window AS (
  SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_in_window
  FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') AND order_date >= '2024-01-01'
  GROUP BY customer_id
)
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT fiw.customer_id) AS customers_in_window, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN fe.first_order_date < '2024-01-01' THEN fiw.customer_id END) AS misclassified_as_new FROM first_in_window fiw JOIN first_ever fe ON fiw.customer_id = fe.customer_id
inflation_causes.fix: Always compute first_order_date from the FULL order history (no date filter), then join that to the current period's orders for classification.

inflation_causes.cause: Account re-creation after deletion or migration
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If a customer deletes their account and re-registers, or if a platform migration creates new customer_ids for existing users, these customers appear as 'new' in the classification. A data migration can cause a one-time surge of false new customers.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('week', c.created_at) AS registration_week, COUNT(DISTINCT c.customer_id) AS new_registrations, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN c.email IN (SELECT email FROM customers GROUP BY email HAVING COUNT(*) > 1) THEN c.customer_id END) AS duplicate_email_registrations FROM customers c GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY new_registrations DESC LIMIT 20
inflation_causes.fix: During migrations, maintain a mapping of old_customer_id to new_customer_id and use the earliest first_order_date across all linked IDs for classification.

deflation_causes.cause: Returning customers misclassified as new due to identity fragmentation
deflation_causes.how_it_deflates: The returning-customer count is deflated (and new is inflated) when identity resolution is poor. This is the mirror of the inflation cause — the same fragmentation that inflates 'new' deflates 'returning'.
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT LOWER(c.email) AS normalized_email, COUNT(DISTINCT c.customer_id) AS distinct_ids, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS total_orders FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY LOWER(c.email) HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT c.customer_id) > 1 ORDER BY total_orders DESC LIMIT 20
deflation_causes.fix: Build a resolved_customer_id column that merges all customer_ids sharing the same email/phone. Use this for new-vs-returning classification.

diagnostic_questions: Is the first_order_date computed across all history, or only within the reporting window?
What percentage of orders come from guest checkout, and are those guests being matched to existing accounts?
Did a platform migration or account merge event happen recently that would re-set customer IDs?
Is the shift in new vs returning driven by a change in acquisition spend, or a change in retention?
Are subscription renewal orders included, and should they be classified as 'returning' orders?

causal_relationships.if: new customer share rising, ec_gmv also rising
causal_relationships.then: growth is acquisition-driven — verify marketing spend increased and check if these new customers have lower ec_clv than historical cohorts

causal_relationships.if: new customer share rising, ec_gmv flat
causal_relationships.then: new customers are replacing churning returning customers at roughly the same rate — the business is on a treadmill, not growing

causal_relationships.if: returning customer share rising, ec_repeat_rate also rising
causal_relationships.then: genuine retention improvement — customers are buying more often, shifting the mix toward returning

causal_relationships.if: new customer share drops suddenly
causal_relationships.then: acquisition channel disruption — check mkt_cac and marketing spend for the same period to identify which channel declined

cross_metric_signals.and_also: ec_gmv declines but ec_repeat_rate remains stable
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: Acquisition funnel disruption. The returning customer base is healthy (stable repeat rate), but the pipeline feeding new customers has broken — typically a marketing budget cut, a paid channel algorithm change, or a landing page regression that reduced conversion from visitor to first purchase.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', f.first_order_date) AS cohort_month, c.acquisition_channel, COUNT(DISTINCT f.customer_id) AS new_customers FROM (SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY customer_id) f JOIN customers c ON f.customer_id = c.customer_id GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 1 DESC, new_customers DESC

cross_metric_signals.and_also: ec_clv is increasing but total customer count is declining
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: Survivorship bias in the customer base. New acquisition has slowed or stopped, so the active customer pool is increasingly composed of the most loyal repeat buyers. CLV rises because only high-value customers remain, but the business is slowly shrinking as these customers eventually churn without replacement.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('quarter', o.order_date) AS quarter, COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id) AS active_customers, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_date = f.first_order_date THEN o.customer_id END) AS new_customers, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS total_gmv, SUM(CASE WHEN o.order_date > f.first_order_date THEN oi.quantity * oi.unit_price ELSE 0 END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price), 0) AS returning_revenue_pct FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id JOIN (SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY customer_id) f ON o.customer_id = f.customer_id WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1

anti_patterns.pattern: Classifying new vs returning based on the reporting window only
anti_patterns.why_wrong: If you check whether a customer has prior orders only within the current quarter, any customer whose first order was last quarter will be labeled 'new' this quarter. This inflates new customer counts in every period and makes retention appear weaker than it is.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT CASE WHEN rn = 1 THEN 'new' ELSE 'returning' END AS customer_type, COUNT(*) FROM (SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date) AS rn FROM orders WHERE order_date BETWEEN '2024-04-01' AND '2024-06-30' AND status NOT IN ('cancelled','test','fraud')) x GROUP BY 1
anti_patterns.good_sql:
WITH first_orders AS (
  SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date
  FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  GROUP BY customer_id
)
SELECT
  CASE WHEN o.order_date = f.first_order_date THEN 'new' ELSE 'returning' END AS customer_type,
  COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id) AS customers
FROM orders o
JOIN first_orders f ON o.customer_id = f.customer_id
WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  AND o.order_date BETWEEN '2024-04-01' AND '2024-06-30'
GROUP BY 1

anti_patterns.pattern: Counting orders instead of customers for the new/returning split
anti_patterns.why_wrong: If you count orders, returning customers are over-represented because they contribute multiple orders each. A business with 100 new customers (1 order each) and 50 returning customers (3 orders each) would show 100 vs 150, making it appear returning-heavy. But by customer count it is 67% new.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT CASE WHEN o.order_date = f.first_order_date THEN 'new' ELSE 'returning' END AS customer_type, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS order_count FROM orders o JOIN (SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled','test','fraud') GROUP BY customer_id) f ON o.customer_id = f.customer_id GROUP BY 1
anti_patterns.good_sql: SELECT CASE WHEN o.order_date = f.first_order_date THEN 'new' ELSE 'returning' END AS customer_type, COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id) AS customers, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS orders, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id JOIN (SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled','test','fraud') GROUP BY customer_id) f ON o.customer_id = f.customer_id WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled','test','fraud') GROUP BY 1

sql_assets.basic_sql:
WITH first_orders AS (
  SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date
  FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  GROUP BY customer_id
)
SELECT
  CASE WHEN o.order_date = f.first_order_date THEN 'new' ELSE 'returning' END AS customer_type,
  COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id) AS customers
FROM orders o
JOIN first_orders f ON o.customer_id = f.customer_id
WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  AND o.order_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-03-31'
GROUP BY 1
sql_assets.intermediate_sql:
WITH first_orders AS (
  SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date
  FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  GROUP BY customer_id
)
SELECT
  DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS month,
  CASE WHEN o.order_date = f.first_order_date THEN 'new' ELSE 'returning' END AS customer_type,
  COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id) AS customers,
  COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS orders,
  SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue,
  SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id), 0) AS aov
FROM orders o
JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
JOIN first_orders f ON o.customer_id = f.customer_id
WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
GROUP BY 1, 2
ORDER BY 1, 2
sql_assets.advanced_sql:
WITH first_orders AS (
  -- Grain: one row per customer. Full history scan to find true first order.
  SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date
  FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  GROUP BY customer_id
),
monthly_split AS (
  SELECT
    DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS month,
    CASE WHEN o.order_date = f.first_order_date THEN 'new' ELSE 'returning' END AS customer_type,
    COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id) AS customers,
    SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  JOIN first_orders f ON o.customer_id = f.customer_id
  WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  GROUP BY 1, 2
),
pivoted AS (
  SELECT
    month,
    COALESCE(SUM(CASE WHEN customer_type = 'new' THEN customers END), 0) AS new_customers,
    COALESCE(SUM(CASE WHEN customer_type = 'returning' THEN customers END), 0) AS returning_customers,
    COALESCE(SUM(CASE WHEN customer_type = 'new' THEN revenue END), 0) AS new_revenue,
    COALESCE(SUM(CASE WHEN customer_type = 'returning' THEN revenue END), 0) AS returning_revenue
  FROM monthly_split
  GROUP BY 1
)
SELECT
  month,
  new_customers,
  returning_customers,
  ROUND(new_customers * 100.0 / NULLIF(new_customers + returning_customers, 0), 2) AS new_customer_pct,
  new_revenue,
  returning_revenue,
  ROUND(returning_revenue * 100.0 / NULLIF(new_revenue + returning_revenue, 0), 2) AS returning_revenue_pct,
  LAG(new_customers) OVER (ORDER BY month) AS prev_month_new,
  ROUND((new_customers - LAG(new_customers) OVER (ORDER BY month)) * 100.0 / NULLIF(LAG(new_customers) OVER (ORDER BY month), 0), 2) AS new_customer_mom_pct
FROM pivoted
ORDER BY month

title: RFM Segmentation Score
when_to_use: segmenting customers for targeted marketing campaigns
identifying at-risk high-value customers before they churn
prioritizing retention outreach by customer value tier
building audience segments for email or ad platforms
understanding the distribution of customer value across the base
business_definition: A composite scoring system that ranks each customer on three dimensions: Recency (days since last purchase), Frequency (number of distinct orders), and Monetary (total revenue). Each dimension is scored 1-5 using quintiles (NTILE(5)), where 5 is the best. The combined RFM score is the concatenation or sum of the three individual scores. Excludes cancelled, test, and fraud-flagged orders.

metric_nature.what_it_measures: A multi-dimensional customer value ranking. Answers: who are my best, most engaged customers, and who is at risk of churning?
metric_nature.what_it_does_not_measure: Future customer behavior. RFM is backward-looking — it tells you what customers have done, not what they will do. A customer scored 5-5-5 today may churn tomorrow if they had a bad experience. Pair with predictive churn models for forward-looking signals.
metric_nature.common_misconception: RFM scores are not comparable across different time windows or customer bases without re-running the quintile calculation. An RFM score of 555 in a dataset of 1000 customers means something different than 555 in a dataset of 100,000 customers. The scores are relative ranks, not absolute measures.

template:
WITH customer_rfm AS (
  SELECT
    o.customer_id,
    CURRENT_DATE - MAX(o.order_date) AS recency_days,
    COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS frequency,
    SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS monetary
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
    AND o.order_date BETWEEN {start_date} AND {end_date}
  GROUP BY o.customer_id
),
rfm_scored AS (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    recency_days,
    frequency,
    monetary,
    NTILE(5) OVER (ORDER BY recency_days DESC) AS r_score,
    NTILE(5) OVER (ORDER BY frequency ASC) AS f_score,
    NTILE(5) OVER (ORDER BY monetary ASC) AS m_score
  FROM customer_rfm
)
SELECT
  customer_id, recency_days, frequency, monetary,
  r_score, f_score, m_score,
  r_score * 100 + f_score * 10 + m_score AS rfm_combined
FROM rfm_scored

inflation_causes.cause: Cancelled orders inflating frequency and monetary scores
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If cancelled orders are included, a customer who placed 5 orders but cancelled 3 has a frequency score based on 5, not 2. Their monetary score also includes the value of orders that were never fulfilled, pushing them into a higher quintile than their actual behavior warrants.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT o.customer_id, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS total_orders, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') THEN o.order_id END) AS completed_orders, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS total_value, SUM(CASE WHEN o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') THEN oi.quantity * oi.unit_price ELSE 0 END) AS completed_value FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id GROUP BY o.customer_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) != COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') THEN o.order_id END) ORDER BY total_value - completed_value DESC LIMIT 20
inflation_causes.fix: Add WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') to the base CTE that computes recency, frequency, and monetary values.

inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: Auto-renewal orders artificially inflate the frequency dimension for subscribers. A customer with a monthly subscription for 12 months has frequency = 12, which pushes them into the top quintile — but this does not reflect 12 discrete purchase decisions.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT o.customer_id, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS total_orders, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.is_subscription = true THEN o.order_id END) AS subscription_orders, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.is_subscription = false THEN o.order_id END) AS non_subscription_orders FROM orders o WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY o.customer_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.is_subscription = true THEN o.order_id END) > 0 ORDER BY subscription_orders DESC LIMIT 20
inflation_causes.fix: Exclude subscription renewal orders from frequency calculation, or compute separate RFM scores for subscription and non-subscription behavior.

inflation_causes.cause: Returns not deducted from monetary value
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If a customer bought $500 of merchandise but returned $400, their monetary score reflects $500 (top quintile), but their actual retained value is $100. The RFM segment suggests a high-value customer when they are actually a serial returner.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT o.customer_id, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS gross_monetary, COALESCE(SUM(r.refund_amount), 0) AS total_refunds, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) - COALESCE(SUM(r.refund_amount), 0) AS net_monetary FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id LEFT JOIN refunds r ON o.order_id = r.order_id WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY o.customer_id HAVING COALESCE(SUM(r.refund_amount), 0) > SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) * 0.3 ORDER BY total_refunds DESC LIMIT 20
inflation_causes.fix: Use net monetary value (gross - refunds) instead of gross monetary value in the M dimension calculation.

deflation_causes.cause: Identity fragmentation splitting one customer into multiple low-scoring profiles
deflation_causes.how_it_deflates: If a single customer has 3 different customer_ids (guest checkout, re-registration, email change), their orders are split across 3 profiles. Each profile has lower frequency and monetary values than the true combined total, pushing all three into lower quintiles.
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT LOWER(c.email) AS normalized_email, COUNT(DISTINCT c.customer_id) AS profiles, SUM(sub.frequency) AS combined_frequency, SUM(sub.monetary) AS combined_monetary FROM customers c JOIN (SELECT customer_id, COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS frequency, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS monetary FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY customer_id) sub ON c.customer_id = sub.customer_id GROUP BY LOWER(c.email) HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT c.customer_id) > 1 ORDER BY combined_monetary DESC LIMIT 20
deflation_causes.fix: Resolve customer identities before RFM scoring. Use a canonical customer_id that merges all known profiles for the same person.

deflation_causes.cause: Recency score deflated by using calendar date instead of business days
deflation_causes.how_it_deflates: If recency is computed as calendar days since last order, weekends and holidays inflate the recency gap. A customer who ordered on Friday and would order again on Monday shows 3 days of recency rather than 1 business day, potentially dropping them a quintile.
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT EXTRACT(DOW FROM o.order_date) AS day_of_week, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS orders FROM orders o WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1
deflation_causes.fix: For most ecommerce contexts, calendar days are appropriate. If the business is B2B with weekday-only ordering patterns, consider using business days for recency or acknowledging the weekend gap in score interpretation.

diagnostic_questions: Are the quintile boundaries stable over time, or do they shift significantly between runs?
Are subscription renewals included in the frequency dimension, and should they be?
Has identity resolution been applied before scoring, or are fragmented profiles being scored independently?
Is the monetary dimension using gross or net revenue (after refunds)?
What time window was used for the RFM calculation — and does it match the business's natural purchase cycle?
Are there extreme outliers (e.g., B2B accounts) that are distorting the quintile boundaries?

causal_relationships.if: high-R-low-F segment is growing
causal_relationships.then: new customers are being acquired but not converting to repeat buyers — check ec_repeat_rate and onboarding flows

causal_relationships.if: low-R-high-F-high-M segment is growing
causal_relationships.then: previously loyal high-value customers are becoming dormant — a churn risk signal that should trigger retention outreach

causal_relationships.if: the 555 segment shrinks while 111 segment grows
causal_relationships.then: overall customer quality is declining — likely driven by acquisition channel mix shifts, verify with ec_new_vs_returning by channel

causal_relationships.if: monetary score distribution compresses (all scores cluster around 3)
causal_relationships.then: the customer base has become more homogeneous in spending — either pricing standardized or outlier B2B accounts were removed

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: The proportion of customers in the 1xx (low recency) RFM segments increases >10%
cross_metric_signals.and_also: ec_cohort_retention shows declining D30/D60 retention in recent cohorts
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: Customer engagement is deteriorating across the board — likely a product, pricing, or experience regression. The RFM recency dimension picks up the same signal as cohort retention: customers are not coming back. The upstream cause is typically a fulfillment quality drop, a competitor entry, or a UX degradation after a product release.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS month, COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id) AS active_customers, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.order_date = sub.last_order AND sub.days_since_prior > 90 THEN o.customer_id END) AS reactivated_customers, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN sub.prev_order_date IS NOT NULL AND CURRENT_DATE - sub.last_order > 90 THEN o.customer_id END) AS going_dormant FROM orders o JOIN (SELECT customer_id, order_date AS last_order, LAG(order_date) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date) AS prev_order_date, order_date - LAG(order_date) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date) AS days_since_prior FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')) sub ON o.customer_id = sub.customer_id AND o.order_date = sub.last_order WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1 DESC LIMIT 12

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: High-monetary RFM segments (xx5) show increasing refund rates
cross_metric_signals.and_also: ec_return_rate is rising in the same period
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: A product quality or catalog issue is affecting the highest-spending customers disproportionately. High-monetary customers often buy more items per order, so quality issues that cause returns hit them harder. This inflates their gross monetary score while masking that their net value is declining.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT p.category, COUNT(DISTINCT r.refund_id) AS refund_count, SUM(r.refund_amount) AS refund_value, SUM(r.refund_amount) * 100.0 / NULLIF(SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price), 0) AS refund_rate_pct FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id LEFT JOIN refunds r ON o.order_id = r.order_id JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') AND o.order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '90 days' GROUP BY p.category ORDER BY refund_rate_pct DESC

anti_patterns.pattern: Using fixed thresholds instead of quintiles for RFM scoring
anti_patterns.why_wrong: Hard-coded thresholds (e.g., 'recency < 30 days = score 5') do not adapt to the business's actual distribution. A luxury goods store where average purchase cycle is 180 days would classify nearly everyone as low-recency with a 30-day threshold, making the segmentation useless.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT customer_id, CASE WHEN recency_days < 30 THEN 5 WHEN recency_days < 60 THEN 4 WHEN recency_days < 90 THEN 3 WHEN recency_days < 180 THEN 2 ELSE 1 END AS r_score FROM customer_rfm
anti_patterns.good_sql: SELECT customer_id, NTILE(5) OVER (ORDER BY recency_days DESC) AS r_score, NTILE(5) OVER (ORDER BY frequency ASC) AS f_score, NTILE(5) OVER (ORDER BY monetary ASC) AS m_score FROM customer_rfm

anti_patterns.pattern: Running RFM on the full customer base including one-time buyers from years ago
anti_patterns.why_wrong: Including ancient single-purchase customers who bought once 5 years ago floods the low-recency quintiles with customers who have effectively churned. This compresses the score distribution for active customers and makes the segmentation less discriminating for actionable marketing decisions.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT customer_id, CURRENT_DATE - MAX(order_date) AS recency_days, COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS frequency, SUM(quantity * unit_price) AS monetary FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled','test','fraud') GROUP BY customer_id
anti_patterns.good_sql:
-- Restrict to customers active in the last 24 months for actionable segmentation
SELECT customer_id, CURRENT_DATE - MAX(order_date) AS recency_days, COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS frequency, SUM(quantity * unit_price) AS monetary FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled','test','fraud') AND o.order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '24 months' GROUP BY customer_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) >= 1

sql_assets.basic_sql: SELECT customer_id, CURRENT_DATE - MAX(order_date) AS recency_days, COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS frequency, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS monetary FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY customer_id ORDER BY monetary DESC LIMIT 100
sql_assets.intermediate_sql:
WITH customer_rfm AS (
  SELECT
    o.customer_id,
    CURRENT_DATE - MAX(o.order_date) AS recency_days,
    COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS frequency,
    SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS monetary
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
    AND o.order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '24 months'
  GROUP BY o.customer_id
)
SELECT
  customer_id, recency_days, frequency, monetary,
  NTILE(5) OVER (ORDER BY recency_days DESC) AS r_score,
  NTILE(5) OVER (ORDER BY frequency ASC) AS f_score,
  NTILE(5) OVER (ORDER BY monetary ASC) AS m_score
FROM customer_rfm
sql_assets.advanced_sql:
WITH customer_rfm AS (
  -- Grain: one row per customer. Only customers active in last 24 months.
  SELECT
    o.customer_id,
    CURRENT_DATE - MAX(o.order_date) AS recency_days,
    COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS frequency,
    SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) - COALESCE(SUM(r.refund_amount), 0) AS net_monetary
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  LEFT JOIN refunds r ON o.order_id = r.order_id
  WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
    AND o.order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '24 months'
  GROUP BY o.customer_id
),
rfm_scored AS (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    recency_days,
    frequency,
    net_monetary,
    NTILE(5) OVER (ORDER BY recency_days DESC) AS r_score,
    NTILE(5) OVER (ORDER BY frequency ASC) AS f_score,
    NTILE(5) OVER (ORDER BY net_monetary ASC) AS m_score
  FROM customer_rfm
),
segment_labeled AS (
  SELECT
    *,
    r_score * 100 + f_score * 10 + m_score AS rfm_combined,
    CASE
      WHEN r_score >= 4 AND f_score >= 4 AND m_score >= 4 THEN 'champions'
      WHEN r_score >= 4 AND f_score >= 3 THEN 'loyal_customers'
      WHEN r_score >= 4 AND f_score <= 2 THEN 'new_customers'
      WHEN r_score <= 2 AND f_score >= 4 AND m_score >= 4 THEN 'at_risk_high_value'
      WHEN r_score <= 2 AND f_score >= 3 THEN 'at_risk'
      WHEN r_score <= 2 AND f_score <= 2 THEN 'lost'
      ELSE 'other'
    END AS segment_label
  FROM rfm_scored
)
SELECT
  segment_label,
  COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS customers,
  ROUND(AVG(recency_days)) AS avg_recency,
  ROUND(AVG(frequency), 1) AS avg_frequency,
  ROUND(AVG(net_monetary), 2) AS avg_monetary,
  ROUND(COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) * 100.0 / NULLIF(SUM(COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id)) OVER (), 0), 2) AS pct_of_base
FROM segment_labeled
GROUP BY segment_label
ORDER BY avg_monetary DESC

title: Cohort Retention Rate
intent_tags: how many customers come back each month
when_to_use: measuring how well the business retains customers over time
comparing retention curves across acquisition cohorts
identifying which months or cohorts have the steepest drop-off
evaluating the impact of a product or experience change on long-term retention
building retention triangles for board-level reporting
determining the natural purchase cycle length
business_definition: For a cohort of customers defined by their first purchase month, the percentage who make at least one additional purchase in each subsequent month (period N). Calculated as COUNT(DISTINCT customers active in period N) / COUNT(DISTINCT customers in cohort) for each N. Excludes cancelled, test, and fraud-flagged orders.

metric_nature.what_it_measures: How customer engagement decays over time after first purchase. Answers: what percentage of customers acquired in January are still buying in March, June, December?
metric_nature.what_it_does_not_measure: Revenue retention. A customer who returns but spends $5 instead of their initial $50 counts as retained. For dollar-based retention, use net revenue retention or cohort revenue curves instead.
metric_nature.common_misconception: Cohort retention is often confused with period-over-period active user counts. Active users can grow while retention declines if acquisition volume is increasing fast enough to mask churn. Retention is a per-cohort metric; total active users is not.

template:
WITH cohort AS (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    DATE_TRUNC('month', MIN(order_date)) AS cohort_month
  FROM orders
  WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  GROUP BY customer_id
),
cohort_activity AS (
  SELECT
    c.cohort_month,
    DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS activity_month,
    COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id) AS active_customers
  FROM orders o
  JOIN cohort c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
  WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
    AND o.order_date BETWEEN {start_date} AND {end_date}
  GROUP BY 1, 2
),
cohort_sizes AS (
  SELECT cohort_month, COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS cohort_size
  FROM cohort
  GROUP BY 1
)
SELECT
  ca.cohort_month,
  (EXTRACT(YEAR FROM ca.activity_month) - EXTRACT(YEAR FROM ca.cohort_month)) * 12 + (EXTRACT(MONTH FROM ca.activity_month) - EXTRACT(MONTH FROM ca.cohort_month)) AS period_number,
  cs.cohort_size,
  ca.active_customers,
  ROUND(ca.active_customers * 100.0 / NULLIF(cs.cohort_size, 0), 2) AS retention_pct
FROM cohort_activity ca
JOIN cohort_sizes cs ON ca.cohort_month = cs.cohort_month
ORDER BY ca.cohort_month, period_number

inflation_causes.cause: Counting order modifications or support-created orders as activity
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If customer service creates replacement or adjustment orders that appear as new order_ids, the customer shows as 'active' in a period when they did not actually make a purchase decision. This inflates retention in later periods.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT o.order_source, DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS month, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS orders, COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id) AS customers FROM orders o WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 2 DESC, 1
inflation_causes.fix: Filter to order_source IN ('web', 'app', 'api') and exclude 'cs_replacement', 'manual', 'adjustment' sources from retention calculations.

inflation_causes.cause: Subscription renewals counted as retention activity
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If monthly subscription renewal orders are included, every subscriber appears retained in every month they are subscribed. This makes retention curves appear flat and high, but it reflects billing automation rather than organic repurchase decisions.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS month, CASE WHEN o.is_subscription = true THEN 'subscription' ELSE 'one_time' END AS order_type, COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id) AS active_customers FROM orders o WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 1, 2
inflation_causes.fix: Either exclude subscription orders (WHERE is_subscription = false) or report subscription and organic retention separately. If subscription is the primary business model, track subscription-specific retention (months subscribed) instead.

inflation_causes.cause: Customer identity merges retroactively inflating early cohorts
inflation_causes.how_it_inflates: If an identity resolution system merges two previously separate customer_ids into one after the fact, the merged customer now appears active in all months that either original ID was active. This retroactively inflates retention for earlier cohorts without any actual behavioral change.
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', c.merged_at) AS merge_month, COUNT(DISTINCT c.customer_id) AS merged_customers FROM customers c WHERE c.merged_at IS NOT NULL GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1 DESC LIMIT 12
inflation_causes.fix: Maintain a consistent identity resolution snapshot for retention reporting. Re-run the retention calculation from scratch after merges rather than patching individual cohorts.

deflation_causes.cause: Guest checkout fragmenting repeat visits across multiple IDs
deflation_causes.how_it_deflates: A customer who orders in Month 0 as a registered user and returns in Month 3 via guest checkout gets a new customer_id. The Month 3 activity is attributed to a new cohort rather than counted as retention in the original cohort. This understates retention across the board.
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT CASE WHEN c.is_guest = true THEN 'guest' ELSE 'registered' END AS type, DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS month, COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id) AS customers FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 2, 1
deflation_causes.fix: Apply identity resolution before computing cohort retention. Map guest customer_ids to their canonical registered profile using email or shipping address matching.

deflation_causes.cause: Using too-fine time granularity for a low-frequency purchase category
deflation_causes.how_it_deflates: If retention is measured monthly but the product category has a 6-month natural repurchase cycle (furniture, mattresses, appliances), nearly all customers will show 0% retention in months 1-5 even though they intend to buy again. The retention curve looks catastrophic because the time unit is wrong.
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT ROUND(AVG(days_between_orders)) AS avg_days_between, PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY days_between_orders) AS median_days_between FROM (SELECT customer_id, order_date, LEAD(order_date) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date) - order_date AS days_between_orders FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')) sub WHERE days_between_orders IS NOT NULL
deflation_causes.fix: Set the retention period granularity to match the natural purchase cycle. If median days-between-orders is 120, use quarterly cohort retention instead of monthly.

diagnostic_questions: What time granularity is being used for cohort periods, and does it match the product's natural purchase cycle?
Are subscription renewal orders included in the retention calculation?
Has identity resolution been applied before assigning customers to cohorts?
Is the retention drop concentrated in period 1 (first-to-second purchase) or distributed across later periods?
Did a specific cohort month show anomalous retention — and was there a promotion or product change in that month?
Are replacement orders or customer-service-created orders being counted as retention activity?

causal_relationships.if: cohort retention up, ec_clv also up
causal_relationships.then: genuine improvement — customers are staying longer and generating more lifetime revenue

causal_relationships.if: cohort retention flat, ec_repeat_rate up
causal_relationships.then: existing repeat customers are buying more frequently, but the rate of first-to-second conversion has not improved — the improvement is in frequency, not breadth of retention

causal_relationships.if: cohort retention down in period 1 only
causal_relationships.then: the first-purchase experience is failing to drive a second purchase — check product quality, shipping experience (logi_on_time_delivery_rate), or post-purchase email sequences

causal_relationships.if: cohort retention declining for recent cohorts but stable for older ones
causal_relationships.then: acquisition quality has degraded — recent cohorts are lower-intent customers, possibly from new channels or heavy discounting

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: Period 1 retention drops >5pp for cohorts acquired in the last quarter
cross_metric_signals.and_also: ec_new_vs_returning shows new customer share spiking in the same quarter
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: Aggressive acquisition campaign brought in low-quality customers. The spike in new customers (high volume) combined with the retention drop (low quality) points to a channel or promotion that attracted one-time deal seekers. Both signals originate from the same upstream cause: a change in acquisition strategy that prioritized volume over intent.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', f.first_order_date) AS cohort_month, c.acquisition_channel, COUNT(DISTINCT f.customer_id) AS cohort_size, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o2.customer_id IS NOT NULL THEN f.customer_id END) AS retained_period_1, ROUND(COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o2.customer_id IS NOT NULL THEN f.customer_id END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT f.customer_id), 0), 2) AS p1_retention_pct FROM (SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY customer_id) f JOIN customers c ON f.customer_id = c.customer_id LEFT JOIN orders o2 ON f.customer_id = o2.customer_id AND o2.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') AND o2.order_date BETWEEN f.first_order_date + INTERVAL '1 month' AND f.first_order_date + INTERVAL '2 months' GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 1 DESC, p1_retention_pct ASC

cross_metric_signals.if_this_metric: Overall retention curve shape changes — flattens earlier or drops off faster
cross_metric_signals.shared_root_cause: A product, pricing, or experience change is causing previously loyal customers to disengage. The retention curve captures this as a shape change across cohorts, while RFM captures it as high-value customers falling into low-recency segments. The upstream cause is typically a competitor launch, a major UX regression, a pricing increase, or a fulfillment quality decline.
cross_metric_signals.diagnostic_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS month, AVG(CASE WHEN o.order_date - LAG(o.order_date) OVER (PARTITION BY o.customer_id ORDER BY o.order_date) IS NOT NULL THEN o.order_date - LAG(o.order_date) OVER (PARTITION BY o.customer_id ORDER BY o.order_date) END) AS avg_days_between_orders, COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id) AS active_customers, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN prev.prior_orders >= 3 THEN o.customer_id END) AS loyal_customers_active FROM orders o LEFT JOIN (SELECT customer_id, order_date, COUNT(*) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING) AS prior_orders FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')) prev ON o.customer_id = prev.customer_id AND o.order_date = prev.order_date WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1 DESC LIMIT 12

anti_patterns.pattern: Using active user count instead of per-cohort retention
anti_patterns.why_wrong: Total active users can grow even while retention declines, as long as new acquisition outpaces churn. This masks a retention problem. Cohort retention isolates each acquisition group and measures their specific decay curve, revealing the true retention picture.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS month, COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS active_customers FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud') GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1
anti_patterns.good_sql:
WITH cohort AS (
  SELECT customer_id, DATE_TRUNC('month', MIN(order_date)) AS cohort_month
  FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  GROUP BY customer_id
)
SELECT
  c.cohort_month,
  DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS activity_month,
  COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id) AS active_customers,
  (SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) FROM cohort WHERE cohort_month = c.cohort_month) AS cohort_size
FROM orders o
JOIN cohort c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
GROUP BY 1, 2
ORDER BY 1, 2

anti_patterns.pattern: Including the cohort month itself (period 0) as 100% and claiming high retention
anti_patterns.why_wrong: Period 0 is by definition 100% — every customer in the cohort was active in their acquisition month. The meaningful retention signal starts at period 1. If you report 'Month 1 retention is 100%', you are reporting the trivial base case, not actual retention.
anti_patterns.bad_sql:
-- This includes period 0 which is always 100%
SELECT cohort_month, period_number, retention_pct FROM retention_table WHERE period_number >= 0
anti_patterns.good_sql:
-- Start from period 1 for meaningful retention analysis
SELECT cohort_month, period_number, retention_pct FROM retention_table WHERE period_number >= 1 ORDER BY cohort_month, period_number

anti_patterns.pattern: Computing retention without a date_spine, leaving gaps for zero-activity periods
anti_patterns.why_wrong: If no customer in a cohort is active in period 4, that row is simply missing from the result set rather than showing 0%. This makes the retention curve look better than reality because zero-retention periods are invisible.
anti_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT c.cohort_month, DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS activity_month, COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id) AS active FROM orders o JOIN cohort c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled','test','fraud') GROUP BY 1, 2
anti_patterns.good_sql:
WITH date_spine AS (
  SELECT GENERATE_SERIES(
    (SELECT MIN(cohort_month) FROM cohort),
    CURRENT_DATE,
    INTERVAL '1 month'
  )::DATE AS month
),
cohort_months AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT cohort_month FROM cohort
)
SELECT
  cm.cohort_month,
  ds.month AS activity_month,
  COALESCE(ca.active_customers, 0) AS active_customers
FROM cohort_months cm
CROSS JOIN date_spine ds
LEFT JOIN cohort_activity ca ON cm.cohort_month = ca.cohort_month AND ds.month = ca.activity_month
WHERE ds.month >= cm.cohort_month
ORDER BY cm.cohort_month, ds.month

sql_assets.basic_sql:
WITH cohort AS (
  SELECT customer_id, DATE_TRUNC('month', MIN(order_date)) AS cohort_month
  FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  GROUP BY customer_id
)
SELECT c.cohort_month, DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS activity_month, COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id) AS active_customers
FROM orders o
JOIN cohort c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
GROUP BY 1, 2
ORDER BY 1, 2
sql_assets.intermediate_sql:
WITH cohort AS (
  SELECT customer_id, DATE_TRUNC('month', MIN(order_date)) AS cohort_month
  FROM orders WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  GROUP BY customer_id
),
cohort_sizes AS (
  SELECT cohort_month, COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS cohort_size
  FROM cohort GROUP BY 1
),
activity AS (
  SELECT c.cohort_month, DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS activity_month,
    COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id) AS active_customers
  FROM orders o
  JOIN cohort c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
  WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  GROUP BY 1, 2
)
SELECT
  a.cohort_month,
  a.activity_month,
  (EXTRACT(YEAR FROM a.activity_month) - EXTRACT(YEAR FROM a.cohort_month)) * 12 + (EXTRACT(MONTH FROM a.activity_month) - EXTRACT(MONTH FROM a.cohort_month)) AS period_number,
  cs.cohort_size,
  a.active_customers,
  ROUND(a.active_customers * 100.0 / NULLIF(cs.cohort_size, 0), 2) AS retention_pct
FROM activity a
JOIN cohort_sizes cs ON a.cohort_month = cs.cohort_month
WHERE a.activity_month >= a.cohort_month
ORDER BY a.cohort_month, period_number
sql_assets.advanced_sql:
WITH cohort AS (
  -- Grain: one row per customer with their cohort assignment
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    DATE_TRUNC('month', MIN(order_date)) AS cohort_month
  FROM orders
  WHERE status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  GROUP BY customer_id
),
cohort_sizes AS (
  SELECT cohort_month, COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS cohort_size
  FROM cohort GROUP BY 1
),
period_spine AS (
  -- Generate period numbers 0-12 for a 12-month retention view
  SELECT generate_series(0, 12) AS period_number
),
activity AS (
  SELECT
    c.cohort_month,
    (EXTRACT(YEAR FROM DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date)) - EXTRACT(YEAR FROM c.cohort_month)) * 12
      + (EXTRACT(MONTH FROM DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date)) - EXTRACT(MONTH FROM c.cohort_month)) AS period_number,
    COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id) AS active_customers,
    SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS period_revenue
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  JOIN cohort c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
  WHERE o.status NOT IN ('cancelled', 'test', 'fraud')
  GROUP BY 1, 2
),
retention_matrix AS (
  SELECT
    cs.cohort_month,
    ps.period_number,
    cs.cohort_size,
    COALESCE(a.active_customers, 0) AS active_customers,
    COALESCE(a.period_revenue, 0) AS period_revenue,
    ROUND(COALESCE(a.active_customers, 0) * 100.0 / NULLIF(cs.cohort_size, 0), 2) AS retention_pct
  FROM cohort_sizes cs
  CROSS JOIN period_spine ps
  LEFT JOIN activity a ON cs.cohort_month = a.cohort_month AND ps.period_number = a.period_number
  WHERE cs.cohort_month + (ps.period_number || ' months')::INTERVAL <= CURRENT_DATE
)
SELECT
  cohort_month,
  period_number,
  cohort_size,
  active_customers,
  retention_pct,
  period_revenue,
  ROUND(period_revenue / NULLIF(active_customers, 0), 2) AS revenue_per_active_customer,
  retention_pct - LAG(retention_pct) OVER (PARTITION BY cohort_month ORDER BY period_number) AS retention_dropoff_pp
FROM retention_matrix
ORDER BY cohort_month, period_number
